originalTitle: Angry crowd sets Ebola hospital tents on fire in DR Congo
title: When Sadness Happens: Being Safe from Sickness in DR Congo
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends all across Africa! Your kid-friendly news reporter is here with an important story from a country called DR Congo. Sometimes, grown-ups can get very, very sad, and it can make things tricky. But learning about it helps us all understand and stay safe! Today, we're talking about a serious sickness called Ebola. It’s a very important topic because it helps us learn how to keep ourselves, our families, and our friends healthy and safe. Let's find out more together.

What is Ebola?

Ebola is a kind of sickness that can make people very, very ill. It's like a bad flu, but much, much stronger. When someone has Ebola, they can get fevers, feel very tired, and their body can hurt a lot. It's a sickness that doctors take very seriously. Ebola can spread from one person to another if they touch each other, or if they touch things that a sick person has touched. That's why doctors and nurses wear special suits that cover them from head to toe! These suits are like superhero costumes that protect them from germs. Did you know? Ebola is named after a river in Africa, the Ebola River, where the sickness was first found a long time ago!

Doctors are Heroes!

In places like DR Congo, brave doctors, nurses, and health workers are like superheroes without capes. They work so hard to help people who are sick with Ebola get better. They are very kind and they want to make sure everyone stays healthy. They leave their own families sometimes to go and help others, which is very courageous. They have special training and know exactly what to do to care for people. They help sick people feel more comfortable and give them medicines to try and make the sickness go away. They are truly amazing helpers in our communities. Did you know? Doctors and nurses study for many, many years to learn how to help us when we are sick or hurt! They are very clever and dedicated.

Special Rules for Safety

When someone has a very strong sickness like Ebola, doctors have very important rules. These rules are not to be mean or difficult, but to keep everyone else safe. Imagine a tiny, invisible germ. Even after a person with Ebola passes away, these tiny germs can still be around on their body. If other people touch the body without special protection, those tiny germs can jump to them and make them sick too. So, doctors ask that burials, which are when we say goodbye to someone, are done in a very special, safe way. This stops the sickness from spreading to more people. Did you know? Germs are tiny, tiny living things that we can't see with our eyes, but some of them can make us very sick! We wash our hands to get rid of them.

A Sad Day in DR Congo

Now, let's talk about what happened in DR Congo. There was a person who got sick with Ebola and, sadly, passed away. Their family and friends were very, very sad. Losing someone you love is one of the hardest things in the world. The family wanted to take their loved one's body home for a traditional burial. This is a very important custom for many people. But the doctors knew about the special rules. They had to say "no" to the family taking the body, because it wasn't safe. They knew the germs could still spread. The doctors were trying to protect the family and everyone else in the village from getting sick too. It was a very hard thing for them to do, and even harder for the sad family to hear.

Why Did People Get Upset?

When people are very, very sad and grieving, it's hard for them to understand difficult things. The family and friends were very upset because they loved their relative so much. They wanted to say goodbye in the way they always had. Perhaps they didn't fully understand why the doctors had these new rules. Maybe they didn't trust the doctors, or felt that their traditions were being disrespected. When people are scared or sad, they sometimes don't think clearly. It's a very human thing to feel that way. It’s important to remember that they were feeling a lot of pain and confusion. They were not trying to be bad, they were just very, very sad and probably didn't know what to do.

What Happened Next?

Because of all this sadness and confusion, some people in the crowd became very angry. They made a very, very bad choice. They hurt the very place where doctors were trying to help people. They made a fire and damaged the special tents where sick people were being cared for. This was not a good way to solve the problem. When the tents were burned, it meant that doctors couldn't help other sick people anymore. It made it even harder for everyone to get well and stay safe. It put more people in danger, including the brave health workers. Did you know? Hospitals and special clinics are meant to be safe places where doctors can help people get better without worrying about being hurt or having their tools broken. Hurting them makes it harder for everyone.

Learning to Talk and Trust

This sad story teaches us something very important: it's good to talk and listen to each other. When doctors explain why rules are important, it helps everyone understand. And when families tell doctors how they feel, it helps doctors understand too. Building trust is super important. When communities trust their doctors and health workers, it’s much easier to work together to fight sickness. Everyone wants the same thing: for people to be healthy and safe. We need to listen to the grown-ups who know about keeping us healthy. Did you know? Talking about our feelings and listening carefully to what others say helps us understand each other better and find good solutions to problems!

Working Together for a Healthy Africa

My dear friends, we all want to live in a healthy Africa, don't we? This means we need to help our doctors and nurses. We need to listen to their advice about how to stay safe from sicknesses like Ebola. Following rules helps protect not just one person, but our whole community! Let's remember to be kind to each other, especially when people are sad or confused. Let's learn to talk about problems and trust the people who are trying to help us. Together, we can make sure our homes, our villages, and our countries stay safe and healthy. Did you know? When everyone in a community works together, even big problems like stopping a sickness can become much easier to manage! We are stronger together. Remember to always wash your hands, listen to your parents and teachers, and be a helper in your community. That's all from your kid-friendly news reporter today. Stay safe and keep smiling!
summary: In DR Congo, sad friends and family of someone who passed away from Ebola got very upset because they couldn't bury their loved one in their usual way, and some people damaged a special place where doctors were helping, making it harder to keep everyone safe.
whyMatters: This matters because it teaches us why it's super important to listen to doctors and understand special rules about sickness, so everyone stays safe and healthy, and we can all help each other and our communities grow strong.

originalTitle: Dozens of vehicles burnt as Mali jihadists enforce blockade ahead of Eid holiday
title: News From Mali: Helping Our Friends!
content:
Hello from Africa, Friends!

Hello, super friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you important news from our big, beautiful continent of Africa. Today, we're talking about a country called Mali. Can you say Mali? It's a place with many wonderful people, just like you and me. But right now, some people in Mali are having a little bit of a tough time. Don't worry, we are going to learn about it in a super simple way, and we'll talk about how we can all hope for things to get better! Sometimes, things happen that make it hard for people to get what they need. This is happening in parts of Mali. There's something called a 'blockade'. It's a big, invisible wall that stops trucks and cars from going where they need to go. Imagine if you had a toy car and a big wall suddenly appeared in front of it! That's kind of like a blockade. It stops things from moving freely. This is making life a bit difficult for families in some towns.

What is a Big Blockade?

Let's talk more about this 'blockade'. It's like someone put a big, invisible stop sign on the roads. This stop sign is not a friendly one. It means that big trucks, which usually carry lots of important things, cannot drive on certain roads. These trucks bring food, medicine, and fuel to different towns and cities. When there's a blockade, it means these important trucks cannot get through. It's like a big traffic jam that never ends, and it stops everything from reaching the people who need it. Some grown-ups who are causing trouble have put this blockade in place. They are making it hard for everyone else to live their normal lives. Did you know? Mali is a very big country in West Africa. It has a beautiful desert called the Sahara Desert and a long, winding river called the Niger River! Many people in Mali love to tell stories and play music.

No Yummy Food!

One of the biggest problems with this blockade is that it's stopping yummy food from reaching people. Imagine going to the shop and finding empty shelves! No fresh fruit, no vegetables, no bread, no rice! That would be very sad, wouldn't it? Trucks usually bring all this good food from farms and big cities to smaller towns. But because of the blockade, these food trucks can't get through. This means that families might not have enough to eat. Children might go to bed feeling hungry. It's a very difficult situation when there isn't enough food for everyone to share. We all need good food to grow strong and have lots of energy to play and learn! This makes people worry a lot. Mums and dads want to make sure their children have enough to eat every day. When the shops are empty, it makes them feel very stressed and sad. They want to cook delicious meals, but sometimes they just don't have the ingredients because the trucks can't deliver them. This is a very important reason why blockades are not good.

No Fuel for Cars!

Another big thing that trucks carry is 'fuel'. Fuel is like special food for cars and motorcycles. Without fuel, cars can't drive! Buses can't take people to school or work. Motorbikes can't help people go to the market. So, when the fuel trucks can't get past the blockade, it means there's no fuel in the petrol stations. Imagine your toy car needs batteries to move, and suddenly there are no batteries anywhere! That's what it's like with fuel. When there's no fuel, everything slows down. Doctors can't drive to help people who are sick. Teachers might find it hard to get to school. And it's hard for people to visit their families or friends in other towns. Even the people who deliver water or help clean the streets need fuel for their vehicles. So, a lack of fuel really stops many important things from happening every day. Did you know? Most cars in Africa use petrol or diesel as fuel. These fuels come from deep inside the earth! Big trucks carry them from big storage places to smaller towns for everyone to use.

What About Eid? A Happy Holiday!

This is happening just before a very special time for many people in Mali. It's a big holiday called Eid! Can you say Eid? It's a Muslim holiday, and it's a time for celebration, happiness, and being together with family and friends. Imagine your favourite holiday, like Christmas or a special birthday party. That's how important Eid is! During Eid, families usually prepare special yummy meals, wear new clothes, and share gifts. They visit their grandmas and grandpas, their aunts and uncles, and all their cousins. But with the blockade, it's very hard to get the special food for Eid, or new clothes, or even to travel to see family. This makes the happy holiday time feel a little bit sad for many people. They really want to celebrate and have fun, but it's hard when there are so many challenges. People want to share joy, but the blockade makes it tough to do so.

Why Do We Need Trucks So Much?

Trucks are like giant helpers on wheels! They carry almost everything we use every day. Think about it: your school books, the clothes you wear, the toys you play with, even the bricks that built your house! Many of these things travel on trucks. So, when these big, strong trucks can't go where they need to go, it's a very big problem. They are like the veins and arteries of a country, carrying important things everywhere. Some trucks carry water, others carry building materials, and some even carry special equipment for hospitals. They are super important for keeping life running smoothly in all the towns and villages. Without them, it's very difficult for people to get the supplies they need for their homes, schools, and hospitals. This is why when 'dozens of vehicles' – that means many, many trucks – can't move, it's a big deal for everyone.

What Happens When Trucks Can't Go?

When the trucks can't pass, not only do people not get food and fuel, but other things can happen too. Sometimes, sad things happen to the trucks themselves. Some of the trucks that tried to get through the blockade got damaged. This is not nice at all. It means even fewer trucks are available to help when the roads finally open up again. It makes it even harder to bring good things to the people who need them. We want all trucks and their drivers to be safe so they can do their important jobs. This makes the situation even more difficult for the people trying to help. Imagine if your bike got a flat tire every time you tried to ride it to your friend's house. It would make you sad and stop you from playing. It's a similar feeling for the truck drivers and the people who rely on those trucks.

How Does This Make People Feel?

This whole situation makes people in Mali feel many different things. They might feel worried, especially the mums and dads. They might feel sad because they can't celebrate Eid properly. They might feel frustrated because they can't get food or fuel. But even with all these tough feelings, people in Mali are very strong and hopeful. They know that good people are working hard to make things better. It's important to remember that even when things are difficult, people still try to help each other. Neighbors share what little they have. Friends try to find ways to support each other. This is called 'community spirit', and it's very strong in Africa. People come together to face challenges.

Who Are the Grown-Ups Helping?

Many grown-ups are trying very hard to help. Leaders in Mali and people from other countries are talking and working together. They want to make sure the blockade stops so that food, fuel, and medicine can reach everyone. They want peace, which means everyone can live safely and happily without trouble. These grown-ups are trying to find solutions so that the bad people who caused the blockade will stop, and the roads will be open again for everyone. Did you know? The capital city of Mali is called Bamako! It's a very busy and exciting city where many people live and work. The people of Mali often speak French, as well as many local languages like Bambara.

Let's All Hope for Good Things!

So, what can we do, even though we are far away? We can send our kind thoughts and hopes to the children and families in Mali. We can hope that the blockade will end very soon. We can hope that trucks will be able to deliver yummy food and important fuel again. We can hope that everyone in Mali can celebrate Eid with joy and happiness, surrounded by their loved ones. It's important to remember that even when there are problems, people are always working to make things better. We hope for peace and kindness to spread across Mali. Let's all wish for safe roads, full shops, and happy celebrations for all our friends in Mali. We are all part of one big family on this Earth, and we care about each other. Let's send them our best wishes for a bright and peaceful future!
summary: Some roads in Mali have a big 'blockade' stopping trucks with food and fuel, making it hard for families to prepare for the special Eid holiday, but many grown-ups are working to help.
whyMatters: This is important because families in Mali need food and fuel to live and celebrate, and the blockade is making it very difficult for them to get these important things.

originalTitle: DR Congo cancels World Cup training camp over Ebola outbreak
title: DR Congo cancels World Cup training camp over Ebola outbreak
content: The outbreak, caused by a rare Ebola species, is thought to have caused 139 deaths so far.

title: Ebola treatment hospital burned down in DRC as outbreak spreads

title: Angry crowd sets Ebola hospital tents on fire in DR Congo

title: Iran steps up claim to control Strait of Hormuz
content: Iran publishes a map claiming "armed forces oversight" across more than 22,000 sq km of the waterway.

originalTitle: How Palmer and Foden lost ruthless Tuchel's battle for number 10 roles
title: How Palmer and Foden lost ruthless Tuchel's battle for number 10 roles
content: Two years ago Phil Foden and Cole Palmer were England's future. Now, they cannot even get into the squad amid fierce rivalry for the number 10 role.

originalTitle: Southampton's Eckert backed spying as club conduct deemed 'deplorable'
title: Southampton's Eckert backed spying as club conduct deemed 'deplorable'
content: Southampton's spying was authorised by head coach Tonda Eckert and was a "contrived and determined plan from the top down to gain a competitive advantage", an independent disciplinary panel says.

originalTitle: Ultimate image for Saudi Arabia's football poster boy: Ronaldo wins title
title: Ultimate image for Saudi Arabia's football poster boy: Ronaldo wins title
content: Cristiano Ronaldo wins his first league title with Al-Nassr after three years in Saudi Arabia.
